title: MoHUA's 'Catch the Rain' Campaign: 75 Amrit SAM Cities Get a National-Level Push
meta_description: MoHUA held a national-level consultation with 75 Amrit Shallow Aquifer Management cities under the Catch the Rain campaign. Here's what it means for you.
html_body:
India's Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA) just made a significant move in the country's water conservation story. Under the popular 'Catch the Rain' campaign, the ministry held a national-level consultation with officials from 75 Amrit Shallow Aquifer Management (SAM) cities. If you live in one of these cities — or care about groundwater in urban India — this is worth understanding.

What Is the 'Catch the Rain' Campaign?

The 'Catch the Rain' campaign is a Government of India initiative focused on rainwater harvesting and groundwater recharge. The core idea is simple: instead of letting rainwater run off into drains and get wasted, we should capture it and let it seep back into the ground. This is especially important for Indian cities, where rapid construction and concretisation have drastically reduced the natural absorption of rainwater into the soil. The campaign encourages both government bodies and citizens to create structures — like recharge pits, check dams, and rooftop harvesting systems — that help replenish groundwater. It runs under the broader umbrella of national water conservation goals and has been gaining momentum across urban and rural India alike.

What Is Amrit SAM — Shallow Aquifer Management?

SAM stands for Shallow Aquifer Management, and it is a focused programme under the AMRIT (Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation) framework. Shallow aquifers are underground water-bearing layers that sit relatively close to the surface — these are the ones most directly recharged by rainwater and most directly tapped by borewells and hand pumps in urban neighbourhoods. When cities grow fast, these shallow aquifers get depleted because rainwater can no longer soak into the ground through concrete and tarmac. The SAM programme specifically targets these aquifers in urban areas, working with city administrations to map them, monitor water levels, and implement recharge strategies. By focusing on 75 cities, MoHUA is trying to create a replicable, scalable model for urban groundwater management across the country.

Why a National-Level Consultation — and Why Now?

A national-level consultation (or 'rashtriya star par varta', as the PIB release calls it) means that officials and representatives from all 75 designated SAM cities were brought together — virtually or physically — to share progress, challenges, and best practices. This kind of dialogue is important because water management is deeply local: what works in a city in Rajasthan may need adjustment for a city in Kerala. By creating a common platform, MoHUA allows city-level officials to learn from each other, align their strategies with national goals, and get direct guidance from the central ministry. The timing also matters — consultations like these are typically held ahead of or during the monsoon season, when the 'Catch the Rain' campaign is most active and when groundwater recharge opportunities are at their peak. The exact date of this consultation is abhi confirm nahi hua — readers should check the official PIB link for the precise schedule.

Which 75 Cities Are Involved?

The announcement specifically mentions 75 Amrit SAM cities — a number that is symbolically significant, as it aligns with India's 'Azadi ka Amrit Mahotsav' theme of celebrating 75 years of independence with 75 landmark initiatives. However, the specific list of which 75 cities are included in this SAM programme is abhi confirm nahi hua from the title of this press release alone. If you want to check whether your city is on the list, the full details are available at the official PIB press release linked in our source note below. City municipal corporations and urban local bodies (ULBs) in these cities are the primary implementing agencies, so your local nagar nigam or nagar palika office would also be a good place to ask.

What Does This Mean for Residents of These Cities?

For ordinary residents, this consultation signals that the central government is actively pushing urban local bodies to take groundwater management seriously — and that accountability is being built into the system. In practical terms, this could mean your city administration may soon be rolling out or scaling up rainwater harvesting mandates for new buildings, creating public recharge structures in parks and open spaces, or launching awareness drives about water conservation. If your city is among the 75, it is worth keeping an eye on announcements from your local municipal body about any new schemes, subsidies, or requirements related to rainwater harvesting. Citizens can also proactively install rooftop rainwater harvesting systems — many states already offer incentives or rebates for this, though specific amounts and eligibility rules vary by state and are abhi confirm nahi hua at the central level from this announcement alone.

How MoHUA Is Driving Urban Water Security

MoHUA has been increasingly active in linking urban development with environmental sustainability. The AMRIT mission, under which SAM sits, was originally focused on water supply and sewerage infrastructure in cities, but it has evolved to include groundwater health as a core concern. By conducting national-level consultations, the ministry is essentially creating a feedback loop — cities report what is working and what is not, the centre refines its guidelines, and better policies flow back down to the city level. This kind of structured dialogue is what separates a well-implemented programme from one that exists only on paper. For India's urban groundwater crisis — which is real and worsening in many cities — initiatives like SAM, backed by active ministerial engagement, represent a meaningful step in the right direction.

faq.q: What is the 'Catch the Rain' campaign?
faq.a: It is a Government of India initiative that promotes rainwater harvesting and groundwater recharge, encouraging citizens and government bodies to capture rainwater instead of letting it go to waste.

faq.q: What does SAM mean in the context of this announcement?
faq.a: SAM stands for Shallow Aquifer Management. It focuses on monitoring and recharging the underground water layers closest to the surface in urban areas, which are most affected by city growth and concretisation.

faq.q: Which cities are the 75 Amrit SAM cities?
faq.a: The specific list of 75 cities is abhi confirm nahi hua from this announcement alone. Please check the official PIB press release at the link in our source note for the full list.
MoHUA is the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, the central government ministry responsible for urban development, housing, and related infrastructure in India.

faq.q: How can I find out if my city is part of this programme?
faq.a: Check the official PIB press release linked below, or contact your local municipal corporation (nagar nigam or nagar palika) for information about whether your city is among the 75 Amrit SAM cities.

fact_sources.claim: MoHUA held a national-level consultation with 75 Amrit Shallow Aquifer Management (SAM) cities under the Catch the Rain campaign
Full details including city list, dates, and programme specifics — readers should verify at the official PIB source
